question: What is the primary election system in Hawaii?
question_variants: Are Hawaii primaries open or closed?
Do I have to register with a party to vote in the HI primary?
Hawaii single ballot primary system
answer: Hawaii has an open primary system. Voters are not required to register with a political party. In the primary, voters receive a single ballot containing the primary races for all recognized parties. The voter must select one, and only one, party's primary to participate in, and can only vote for candidates within that selected party section, per HRS § 12-31.
source_name: Hawaii Office of Elections - Voting in Hawaii

question: On what day of the week is the Hawaii primary election held?
question_variants: Is the Hawaii primary on a Saturday?
What day of the week do Hawaiians vote in the primary?
Saturday voting Hawaii
answer: In a major practical distinctive compared to the rest of the nation, Hawaii holds its primary election on a Saturday. By state law (HRS § 12-2), the primary election is held on the second Saturday of August in every even-numbered year.

question: Are write-in candidates permitted in Hawaii?
question_variants: Do write-in votes count in Hawaii?
How to register as a write-in candidate in HI
Blank spaces on Hawaii ballots
answer: No. Hawaii is one of the very few states that explicitly bans write-in voting. Under HRS § 11-152, no write-in votes are permitted or counted in primary, special, or general elections. Candidates must officially qualify and appear printed on the ballot to receive valid votes.

question: What are the petition signature requirements to access the primary ballot in Hawaii?
question_variants: How many signatures do I need to run for office in HI?
Hawaii nomination paper signatures
Petition thresholds for Hawaii legislature
answer: Hawaii has exceptionally low signature thresholds for ballot access. Candidates must file nomination papers containing the signatures of registered voters eligible to vote for that office. U.S. Senate, U.S. House, Governor, and Lt. Governor candidates need only 25 signatures. State legislative candidates (House or Senate) need only 15 signatures (HRS § 12-5).

question: Do the Governor and Lieutenant Governor run on a joint ticket in the Hawaii primary?
question_variants: Hawaii joint ticket rules
How are Lt Gov candidates selected in HI?
Does the Governor pick a running mate in the primary?
answer: No, they do not run jointly in the primary. Under Article V, Section 2 of the Hawaii Constitution, candidates for Governor and Lieutenant Governor run in separate, independent primary elections. The winners of each respective primary are then paired together to run as a single joint ticket representing their party in the November general election.

question: How do nonpartisan candidates get on the general election ballot in Hawaii?
question_variants: Independent candidate ballot access Hawaii
Running without a party in HI
Nonpartisan primary advancement rules Hawaii
answer: Nonpartisan (independent) candidates must run in the state primary on the nonpartisan ballot. To advance to the general election, a nonpartisan candidate must receive votes equal to at least 10% of the total votes cast for that office in the primary, OR receive a vote total equal to or greater than the lowest vote-getting partisan candidate who advanced to the general (HRS § 12-41).

question: Does Hawaii have a sore loser law?
question_variants: Can I run as an independent if I lose the HI primary?
Hawaii sore loser statute
General election ballot access after primary defeat HI
answer: Yes. Under HRS § 12-1(b), Hawaii enforces a sore loser law. A candidate who seeks a party's nomination (or runs as a nonpartisan) in the primary election and is defeated cannot subsequently have their name placed on the general election ballot for the same office or any other office in that election cycle.

question: What is the candidate filing deadline for Hawaii in 2026?
question_variants: When do candidates file for office in Hawaii?
Hawaii filing deadline 2026
Last day to file nomination papers HI
answer: To appear on the 2026 primary ballot, candidates must file their official nomination papers and signatures by 4:30 p.m. on the first Tuesday in June. For the 2026 cycle, this deadline is June 2, 2026.

question: When are the primary and general elections in Hawaii for 2026?
question_variants: Hawaii primary date 2026
When is Election Day 2026 in Hawaii?
August Saturday primary Hawaii
answer: The Hawaii primary election will be held on Saturday, August 8, 2026. The general election will be held on Tuesday, November 3, 2026. Voter Service Centers (for in-person voting) are open from 7:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. on Election Day.

question: Is Governor Josh Green running for re-election in 2026?
question_variants: Hawaii Governor election 2026
Who is defending the HI Governor seat?
Is Josh Green term limited?
answer: Yes, Governor Josh Green (D) is defending his seat in the 2026 election. He was first elected in 2022 and is running for his second term. Since Hawaii Governors are limited to two consecutive terms, he is fully eligible for the 2026 ballot.

question: Which U.S. Senate seats are up for election in Hawaii in 2026?
question_variants: Is Mazie Hirono up for re-election in 2026?
When is Brian Schatz's next election?
Hawaii US Senate cycle 2026
answer: There is no U.S. Senate race in Hawaii in 2026. Senator Brian Schatz (Class 3) was re-elected in 2022 and is not up until 2028. Senator Mazie Hirono (Class 1) was re-elected in 2024 and is not up until 2030.

question: How many U.S. House districts are in Hawaii for the 2026 election?
question_variants: Hawaii congressional apportionment
Are all HI congressional seats up in 2026?
Number of US Representatives from Hawaii
answer: Hawaii is apportioned 2 U.S. House districts. Both District 1 (which includes urban Honolulu) and District 2 (which includes non-Honolulu Oahu and the neighbor islands) will be on the 2026 ballot, as Representatives serve two-year terms.

question: Which Hawaii State Legislature seats are up for election in 2026?
question_variants: Are all Hawaii House seats up in 2026?
Hawaii Senate election cycle 2026
Which legislative districts are on the ballot in HI?
answer: All 51 seats in the Hawaii House of Representatives (2-year terms) are up for election in 2026. The Hawaii Senate has 25 members serving staggered 4-year terms. Due to post-redistricting assignments made in 2022, 13 specific Senate seats will be on the 2026 ballot.

question: Which Office of Hawaiian Affairs (OHA) trustee seats are on the 2026 ballot?
question_variants: OHA election cycle 2026
How many OHA trustees are up for election?
Hawaii Native elections
answer: The Office of Hawaiian Affairs (OHA) is governed by a 9-member Board of Trustees serving staggered four-year terms. In 2026, 5 of the 9 trustee seats will be on the statewide ballot. Following the Supreme Court ruling in Rice v. Cayetano (2000), all registered Hawaii voters may vote in these races.

question: How are judges retained in Hawaii, and are they on the ballot?
question_variants: Hawaii judicial retention elections
How are judges selected in Hawaii?
Do Hawaii judges run in competitive elections?
answer: Hawaii judges do not run in competitive partisan or nonpartisan elections. They are appointed by the Governor (or Chief Justice) from a list provided by the Judicial Selection Commission and confirmed by the State Senate. To serve subsequent terms, they are subject to retention evaluation by the Judicial Selection Commission, NOT by popular vote on the public ballot.

question: What is the schedule for the Hawaii State Legislature sessions?
question_variants: When does the HI legislature meet?
Hawaii 60 day legislative session
Is the Hawaii legislature full time?
answer: The Hawaii State Legislature is a part-time body. Under Article III, Section 10 of the Hawaii Constitution, regular sessions convene annually on the third Wednesday in January and are strictly limited to 60 legislative days, typically concluding in early May.

question: What are the eligibility requirements to run for Governor of Hawaii?
question_variants: Age and residency for Hawaii Governor
Hawaii Constitution Article V Governor qualifications
Who is eligible to be Governor of Hawaii?
answer: Under Article V, Section 1 of the Hawaii Constitution, a candidate for Governor must be at least 30 years of age and must have been a resident of Hawaii for at least five consecutive years immediately preceding the election.

question: What are the qualifications to run for Lieutenant Governor in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii Lt Gov requirements
Does the Lt Gov have the same requirements as the Governor in HI?
Qualifications for Lieutenant Governor of Hawaii
answer: Under Article V, Section 2 of the Hawaii Constitution, the qualifications for Lieutenant Governor are strictly identical to those for Governor: the candidate must be at least 30 years old and a resident of Hawaii for five consecutive years preceding the election.

question: What are the qualifications to run for the Hawaii State Legislature?
question_variants: Qualifications for HI State Representative
Age requirement Hawaii State Senate
Do I have to live in my district to run in HI?
answer: Under Article III, Section 7 of the Hawaii Constitution, a candidate for the State House or State Senate must have attained the age of majority (18 years old), must have been a resident of the state for at least three years, and must be a qualified voter of the representative district from which they seek to be elected prior to filing nomination papers.

question: What are the eligibility requirements for U.S. House and Senate candidates in Hawaii?
question_variants: US Senate residency requirements Hawaii
Age to run for Congress in Hawaii
Federal constitutional requirements for HI candidates
answer: Federal candidates must meet U.S. Constitutional requirements: U.S. House members must be at least 25 years old, a U.S. citizen for 7 years, and an inhabitant of the state when elected. U.S. Senators must be 30 years old, a U.S. citizen for 9 years, and an inhabitant of the state when elected. District residency is not required for the House.

question: What are the term limits for the Hawaii Governor?
question_variants: How many terms can a Hawaii Governor serve?
Josh Green term limits HI
Hawaii executive term limit law
answer: Under Article V, Section 1 of the Hawaii Constitution, the Governor is limited to serving two consecutive four-year terms. After serving two consecutive terms, an individual may run for the office again after a four-year break.

question: Does Hawaii have legislative term limits?
question_variants: How many terms can a Hawaii state senator serve?
Are there term limits for the Hawaii House?
Hawaii legislature term limit status
answer: No. Hawaii does not impose term limits on members of the State Senate or the State House of Representatives. State legislators may serve an unlimited number of terms.

question: Do Office of Hawaiian Affairs (OHA) trustee candidates have to be Native Hawaiian?
question_variants: Qualifications for OHA trustee
Can a non-Hawaiian run for OHA in Hawaii?
Arakaki v. Hawaii OHA restrictions
answer: No. While OHA was established to manage programs for Native Hawaiians, the 9th Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in Arakaki v. Hawaii (2003) that requiring candidates for OHA to be of Native Hawaiian ancestry violated the 15th Amendment and the Voting Rights Act. Therefore, any qualified voter in Hawaii may run for an OHA trustee seat.

question: Is Hawaii a universal mail ballot state?
question_variants: Do all voters get a mail ballot in Hawaii?
Hawaii vote-by-mail system
Does Hawaii automatically send ballots to voters?
answer: Yes. Following legislation enacted in 2021, Hawaii is a universal mail ballot state. All active registered voters are automatically mailed a ballot packet approximately 18 days prior to every primary and general election.
source_name: Hawaii Office of Elections - Voting By Mail

question: Does Hawaii offer early in-person voting?
question_variants: Can I vote early in person in HI?
Hawaii Voter Service Centers
Where can I vote in person in Hawaii?
answer: Yes. While elections are conducted primarily by mail, Hawaii provides 'Voter Service Centers' in each county. These centers open 10 days prior to the election and remain open through Election Day, allowing voters to cast their ballots in person, register to vote, or utilize accessible voting equipment.
source_name: Hawaii Office of Elections - Voter Service Centers

question: What is the deadline to return a mail ballot in Hawaii?
question_variants: When is the last day to mail my ballot in HI?
Hawaii mail ballot postmark deadline
What time are ballots due in Hawaii?
answer: In Hawaii, completed mail ballots must be RECEIVED by the County Clerk's office by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day. Postmarks are not accepted as proof of timely submission; if the ballot is not physically received by the 7:00 p.m. deadline, it will not be counted.

question: Does Hawaii allow same-day voter registration?
question_variants: Late registration Hawaii
Can I register to vote on Election Day in HI?
Hawaii voter registration deadline
answer: Yes. While standard mail and online voter registration deadlines fall 10 days before an election, Hawaii offers Same Day Voter Registration. Eligible residents can register and vote in person at any Voter Service Center in their county from 10 days prior to the election up through the close of polls at 7:00 p.m. on Election Day.

question: Is a strict photo ID required to vote in person in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii voter ID requirements
Do I need my driver's license to vote in HI?
Acceptable ID for Hawaii in-person voting
answer: No. Hawaii does not have a strict photo ID law. Under HRS § 11-136, voters at Voter Service Centers must provide proof of identification, but this can be satisfied by a photo ID (like a driver's license) OR non-photo documents that show the voter's name and address, such as a current utility bill, bank statement, or government check.

question: Which agency administers elections in Hawaii?
question_variants: Does the Secretary of State run elections in Hawaii?
Who is the chief election officer in HI?
Hawaii election administration
answer: In Hawaii, elections are administered by the State Office of Elections under the direction of the Chief Election Officer, rather than a Secretary of State. The state office works in conjunction with the county clerks from Hawaii's four counties to execute elections.

question: What is the electioneering buffer zone distance in Hawaii?
question_variants: How close can I campaign to a Voter Service Center in HI?
Hawaii 200 feet electioneering rule
HI buffer zone around drop boxes
answer: Under HRS § 11-132, Hawaii strictly prohibits electioneering within a 200-foot radius of the entrance to any Voter Service Center or place of deposit (ballot drop box). This includes distributing campaign literature, displaying signs, or soliciting votes.

question: How many counties are in Hawaii, and why does it matter for campaigns?
question_variants: Hawaii county election administration
How many county clerks in HI?
Local election control Hawaii
answer: Hawaii has effectively 4 counties (Honolulu, Maui, Hawaii/Big Island, and Kauai). This highly centralized structure means campaigns only need to coordinate with four County Clerks, who manage local Voter Service Centers, absentee ballot counting, and local election execution on behalf of the state.
source_name: Hawaii Office of Elections - County Divisions

question: Which agency administers campaign finance tracking in Hawaii?
question_variants: Where do I file campaign finance reports in HI?
What is the Hawaii CSC?
Does the Hawaii Office of Elections handle campaign finance?
answer: Campaign finance and disclosure laws in Hawaii are administered by the Campaign Spending Commission (CSC), a separate and independent state agency. All state, legislative, and local candidates must register and file their financial disclosures through the CSC's online portal.

question: What are the individual campaign contribution limits for Hawaii candidates in the 2026 cycle?
question_variants: How much can an individual donate to an HI Governor candidate?
Hawaii legislative contribution caps 2026
HI individual donation limits per election cycle
answer: In Hawaii, contribution limits are applied per election period (cycle), not per individual primary/general election. Individuals may contribute up to $6,000 to a candidate for a 4-year statewide office (Governor, Lt. Gov); $4,000 to a candidate for a 4-year non-statewide office (State Senate); and $2,000 to a candidate for a 2-year office (State House) per HRS § 11-357.

question: Are corporate and union contributions permitted in Hawaii candidate campaigns?
question_variants: Corporate contribution ban Hawaii state
Can a business donate to my campaign in HI?
Corporate campaign finance rules Hawaii
answer: Yes. Hawaii permits direct corporate and labor union contributions to candidate campaigns. Noncandidate committees, corporations, and unions are subject to the exact same contribution limits as individual donors ($6,000 / $4,000 / $2,000 depending on the office's term length).

question: What is the campaign finance reporting schedule for Hawaii candidates?
question_variants: CSC filing dates 2026
When are campaign finance reports due in HI?
Hawaii pre-primary and pre-general report deadlines
answer: Candidates must file periodic reports with the CSC. Standard filings in an election year include a 1st Preliminary Primary report (due 30 days before the primary), a 2nd Preliminary Primary report (due 10 days before the primary), a Final Primary report, corresponding General election reports, and a Supplemental report due January 31.

question: Does Hawaii provide public financing for state campaigns?
question_variants: Public matching funds for Hawaii Governor
Hawaii Election Campaign Fund
Government campaign grants HI
answer: Yes. Hawaii operates a partial public financing system through the Hawaii Election Campaign Fund. Candidates who voluntarily agree to abide by strict campaign expenditure limits can receive matching funds from the state, up to a maximum of 10% of the expenditure limit for their specific office, by matching small qualifying contributions.

question: Are candidates prohibited from using campaign funds for personal use in Hawaii?
question_variants: HI personal use prohibition campaign finance
Can I use campaign money for personal items in HI?
Prohibited expenditures Hawaii CSC
answer: Yes. Under HRS § 11-381, campaign funds may not be used for personal expenses. Contributions may only be used for expenses directly related to the candidate's campaign, for the ordinary and necessary expenses of holding public office, or returned to donors.

question: What are Hawaii's rapid disclosure requirements for late contributions?
question_variants: Hawaii late contribution report
Late contribution reporting Hawaii CSC
When do I file a rapid report in HI?
answer: Under HRS § 11-332.1, if a candidate receives a 'Late Contribution' aggregating more than $500 from a single source within the period between 14 days and 4 days before an election, they must electronically report that contribution to the CSC within three business days of receipt.

question: Who draws Hawaii's legislative and congressional maps?
question_variants: Does Hawaii use an independent redistricting commission?
Who has the authority to draw district lines in Hawaii?
Hawaii Reapportionment Commission composition
answer: Hawaii uses a 9-member Reapportionment Commission to draw its electoral maps. Under Article IV of the state constitution, the Senate President, House Speaker, Senate Minority Leader, and House Minority Leader each select two members. Those eight members then select a ninth member to serve as the chairperson.

question: What are the legal criteria for redistricting in Hawaii?
question_variants: Standards for drawing Hawaii district lines
Hawaii gerrymandering rules
Article IV Hawaii Constitution redistricting
answer: Under Article IV of the Hawaii Constitution, districts must be contiguous, compact, and adhere to the 'basic island units' (preventing districts from crossing island boundaries unless strictly necessary for population equality). District lines must follow permanent recognized features and must not be drawn to unduly favor a person or political faction.

question: Are the current Hawaii redistricting maps operative for the 2026 cycle?
question_variants: Status of 2022 Hawaii redistricting maps
Are there pending redistricting lawsuits in Hawaii?
Hawaii 2026 district boundaries
answer: Yes. The legislative and congressional maps adopted by the Hawaii Reapportionment Commission in 2022 are fully operative for the 2026 election cycle and are not currently subject to any systemic legal challenges that would alter boundaries.

question: Can the Hawaii legislature amend the redistricting commission's maps?
question_variants: Legislature override of Hawaii redistricting
Can HI lawmakers change district boundaries?
Hawaii redistricting amendment rules
answer: No. Once the Reapportionment Commission files its final legislative and congressional reapportionment plans with the Chief Election Officer, those plans become law upon publication. The state legislature has no authority to vote on, amend, or veto the maps.

question: Does Hawaii have a recall mechanism for state officials?
question_variants: Can the Governor of Hawaii be recalled?
How to recall a state senator in HI
Statewide recall election rules Hawaii
answer: No. The State of Hawaii does not have any legal mechanism for the recall of state-level elected officials, including the Governor or members of the state legislature.

question: What are the rules for local recall in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii county commissioner recall rules
Signature requirement local recall Hawaii
Recalling a mayor in HI
answer: While state officials cannot be recalled, Hawaii allows for the recall of local elected officials at the county level (mayors and council members), governed entirely by individual county charters. No specific grounds for recall are required. Signature thresholds vary widely, ranging from 10% to 25% of registered voters depending on the county.

question: What are the signature thresholds for a citizen initiative in Hawaii?
question_variants: How to get an initiative on the HI ballot
Hawaii citizen initiative signature requirements
Does Hawaii allow citizen initiatives?
answer: Hawaii is one of the states that does NOT have a statewide citizen initiative process. Citizens cannot draft statutes or constitutional amendments and gather signatures to force them onto the state ballot. All statewide ballot measures must be referred by the legislature.

question: What is the threshold and process for a veto referendum in Hawaii?
question_variants: How to refer a HI bill to the ballot
Hawaii citizen referendum rules
Veto referendum signature threshold Hawaii state
answer: Hawaii does not have a statewide veto referendum process. Citizens cannot gather signatures to suspend and refer an already-passed legislative act to the ballot for a popular vote of approval or rejection.

question: How does the Hawaii legislature refer a constitutional amendment to the voters?
question_variants: Legislative referral process Hawaii
Referendum by legislature HI
Can the HI legislature put a constitutional amendment on the ballot?
answer: The Hawaii State Legislature can propose a constitutional amendment by securing a two-thirds supermajority vote in both the House and the Senate during a single session, OR by securing a simple majority vote in both chambers across two successive sessions. The amendment is then referred to the voters and does not require the Governor's signature.

question: What voter approval threshold is required to pass ballot measures in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii ballot measure pass threshold
Does a measure need a supermajority in HI?
Majority vote requirements Hawaii ballot measures
answer: In Hawaii, a legislatively referred constitutional amendment must be approved by a majority of all the votes tallied upon the question, provided that this 'yes' vote majority constitutes at least 50% of the TOTAL vote cast at the election. If many voters skip the question (blank votes), it essentially counts against the measure.

question: Is the Hawaii Attorney General an elected office?
question_variants: Running for HI Attorney General
Who elects the Attorney General in Hawaii?
Legal requirements for Hawaii Attorney General
answer: No. The Attorney General of Hawaii is not elected by the voters. The Attorney General serves as the state's chief legal officer and is appointed directly by the Governor, subject to confirmation by the State Senate.
source_name: Hawaii Department of the Attorney General

question: What are the eligibility requirements to run for Secretary of State in Hawaii?
question_variants: Requirements to run for HI Secretary of State
Who can run for Secretary of State in HI?
Does Hawaii have a Secretary of State?
answer: Hawaii DOES NOT have a Secretary of State. It is one of the few states where this office does not exist. Election administration duties are handled by the Chief Election Officer (overseeing the Office of Elections), and executive duties typically handled by a Secretary of State are assigned to the Lieutenant Governor.

question: What are the eligibility requirements for County Mayor in Hawaii?
question_variants: Qualifications for Hawaii County Mayor
Can I run for mayor in HI?
Residency rules for Hawaii mayors
answer: To hold the office of county mayor in Hawaii, candidates must meet qualifications set by their specific county charter. Generally, a candidate must be at least 30 years of age (or 25 depending on the county), a registered voter, and a resident of the county for at least 1 to 3 years prior to the election.
source_name: Hawaii Office of Elections - Candidate Qualifications

question: What are the eligibility requirements for a school board candidate in Hawaii?
question_variants: Run for school board Hawaii requirements
Who can be a school trustee in HI?
School board residency rules Hawaii
answer: Hawaii is unique as the ONLY state with a single statewide public school district. There are no local school boards or elected school trustees. Following a constitutional amendment, the statewide Board of Education members are APPOINTED by the Governor, so there are no school board candidates.

question: How are Hawaii Supreme Court justices selected and what are the qualifications?
question_variants: Hawaii Supreme Court Justice eligibility
Years of practice required for HI Supreme Court
Are Hawaii judges elected or appointed?
answer: Hawaii Supreme Court justices are not elected. They are nominated by the Governor from a list of 4 to 6 names submitted by the Judicial Selection Commission and confirmed by the State Senate. To be eligible, a candidate must be a resident, a U.S. citizen, and licensed to practice law by the Hawaii Supreme Court for at least 10 years.

question: What is the ballot signature cure process for absentee ballots in Hawaii?
question_variants: How to fix a rejected signature in HI
Hawaii ballot cure process deadline
Missing signature on HI mail ballot
answer: Because Hawaii is a universal mail-in state, signature verification is critical. If a return envelope is unsigned or the signature does not match the voter's registration record, the county clerk notifies the voter. The voter has up to 5 days after Election Day (by 5:00 p.m. on the 5th day) to cure the discrepancy for their vote to count.

question: What are the deadlines for military and overseas (UOCAVA) voters in Hawaii?
question_variants: When are military ballots due in Hawaii?
UOCAVA return deadline HI
Overseas voting rules Hawaii state
answer: Under UOCAVA, ballots are transmitted to military and overseas voters at least 45 days before the election. These voters may return their voted ballots via mail, email, or fax. The voted ballot must be received by the county clerk's office by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day.

question: When are provisional ballots used in Hawaii?
question_variants: What is a provisional ballot in HI?
Voting without ID Hawaii
When do election workers issue provisional ballots?
answer: Provisional ballots are rarely used in Hawaii because the state operates entirely by mail and offers Same-Day Registration at Voter Service Centers. They are generally only issued in rare edge cases involving federal elections where a voter's eligibility cannot be immediately verified at the service center.

question: Does Hawaii have Automatic Voter Registration (AVR) at the DMV?
question_variants: Is registration automatic at the Hawaii MVD?
HI motor voter automatic registration
Do I have to register to vote if I get a HI driver's license?
answer: Yes. Hawaii implemented Automatic Voter Registration (AVR) beginning in 2021. Eligible citizens who apply for or renew a driver's license or state ID card at the Department of Transportation are automatically registered to vote or have their address updated, unless they affirmatively decline (opt-out).

question: Does Hawaii offer Online Voter Registration?
question_variants: Can I register to vote online in HI?
Hawaii electronic voter registration
HI Office of Elections online registration portal
answer: Yes. Eligible Hawaii residents can register to vote or update their registration using the State of Hawaii Online Voter Registration system. To use the online portal, voters must have a valid Hawaii Driver's License or State ID and their Social Security Number.

question: How does Hawaii maintain its voter registration lists?
question_variants: HI voter list maintenance rules
How are inactive voters handled in Hawaii?
Hawaii voter roll cleaning process
answer: Hawaii conducts list maintenance using National Change of Address (NCOA) data and returned mail. If a mail ballot is returned as undeliverable, the voter is flagged as inactive. If an inactive voter does not respond to confirmation notices or vote over subsequent election cycles, their registration is canceled.

question: How are voting rights restored for individuals with felony convictions in Hawaii?
question_variants: Can felons vote in Hawaii state?
Felony voting rights restoration HI
Voting on parole in Hawaii
answer: In Hawaii, a person convicted of a felony only loses their right to vote while they are actively incarcerated in a correctional facility serving their sentence. Voting rights are automatically restored immediately upon their final discharge or release. Individuals on probation or parole are fully eligible to vote.

question: Does Hawaii use runoff elections for close races?
question_variants: Are there runoffs in Hawaii primaries?
How are tied elections decided in Hawaii?
Tie-breaker rules Hawaii elections
answer: No. Hawaii does not hold runoff elections for state or federal offices. Plurality vote determines the winner. In the rare event of an exact tie between candidates, state law dictates that the chief election officer or county clerk determines the winner by casting lots (e.g., drawing names or flipping a coin).

question: Is Ranked Choice Voting (RCV) used in Hawaii?
question_variants: Does Hawaii use ranked choice voting?
RCV status in Hawaii 2026
Instant runoff voting HI
answer: Ranked Choice Voting (RCV) is not used for standard state or federal elections in Hawaii. Plurality voting is the standard. However, the Hawaii legislature has authorized the use of RCV for specific special federal elections (vacancies) to avoid the cost of traditional special primary runoff elections.

question: What disclaimer language is required on Hawaii campaign materials?
question_variants: Hawaii 'Paid for by' rules
Campaign ad disclaimer requirements HI
Political taglines Hawaii CSC
answer: Hawaii requires that all campaign advertisements clearly contain a disclaimer identifying who paid for the ad. The disclaimer must include the name and address of the candidate, committee, or individual paying for the communication. If an ad is not authorized by a candidate, it must prominently display a notice that the advertisement is 'without the approval and authority' of the candidate.
source_name: Hawaii Campaign Spending Commission - Advisory Opinion 04-03

question: When does outside spending become prohibited coordination in Hawaii?
question_variants: HI campaign coordination rules
When is a PAC expenditure not independent in Hawaii?
Prohibition on candidate-PAC coordination HI
answer: An expenditure is 'coordinated' (and thus treated as an in-kind contribution subject to statutory limits) if it is made in cooperation, consultation, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of, a candidate or their committee. Foreign nationals and corporations with significant foreign ownership are strictly prohibited from coordinating or spending money in Hawaii elections.

question: What are the penalties for late campaign finance filings in Hawaii?
question_variants: HI CSC late fee
Penalty for missing a Hawaii campaign finance deadline
Campaign Spending Commission fine for late filing HI
answer: The Campaign Spending Commission assesses fines of $50 per day for the first seven days a report is late, and $200 per day thereafter. The fine is capped at 25% of the total contributions or expenditures for the reporting period, with a minimum fine of $200 if the report is more than four days late.

question: When and how must a Hawaii campaign committee terminate?
question_variants: Closing a Hawaii campaign committee
CSC committee termination requirements
Final campaign finance report Hawaii
answer: A candidate committee may formally terminate and close its registration with the Campaign Spending Commission only when it has no surplus funds and no outstanding campaign debts or loans. A final closing report must be filed.

question: What are the disclosure rules for independent expenditures in Hawaii?
question_variants: HI independent expenditure reporting CSC
Disclosure for HI independent expenditure groups
Reporting thresholds for outside spending Hawaii
answer: Noncandidate committees making independent expenditures to advocate for or against a candidate must file regular reports with the CSC. Furthermore, if they make independent expenditures of more than $500 within 14 to 4 days prior to an election, they must file a late expenditure report three days before the election.

question: How are in-kind contributions valued and reported in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii in-kind contribution valuation rules
Reporting non-monetary donations Hawaii campaign
Fair market value for in-kind gifts HI
answer: In-kind contributions (donated goods, services, or equipment) must be reported to the Campaign Spending Commission at their fair market value—the standard price the campaign would have paid. They count toward the donor's per-cycle contribution limit just like monetary cash gifts.

question: Are there limits on a candidate loaning personal funds to their own campaign in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii candidate personal loan limits
Can I pay myself back for a campaign loan in HI?
Reporting candidate loans in CSC
answer: Candidates may contribute completely unlimited amounts of their personal funds to their own campaign (exempt from limits). However, if the candidate makes a personal loan to the campaign with the intent of repayment, they cannot be repaid more than $50,000 for Governor/Lt. Governor, $25,000 for Mayor, or $10,000 for all other offices from campaign funds.

question: What can Hawaii candidates do with unexpended campaign funds?
question_variants: What can I do with leftover campaign money in HI?
HI surplus campaign funds rules
Donating remaining campaign funds Hawaii
answer: Unexpended campaign funds cannot be converted to personal use. They may be returned pro-rata to contributors, transferred to another candidate committee, donated to a public school or public library, or donated to an IRS-recognized charitable organization.

question: Is there a session freeze on campaign fundraising for Hawaii legislators?
question_variants: Can Hawaii legislators hold fundraisers during session?
Lobbyist contribution ban HI legislature
Legislative session fundraiser rules Hawaii
answer: Yes. Under HRS § 11-342, Hawaii enforces a 'session freeze' that prohibits the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, and state legislators from holding any political fundraisers during the regular legislative session, ensuring money is not actively solicited at events while bills are moving.

question: What is the expenditure limit trade-off for public financing in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii Election Campaign Fund matching rules
Why accept expenditure limits in HI?
How to get public campaign money in Hawaii
answer: Candidates in Hawaii can voluntarily agree to abide by strict campaign expenditure limits (calculated based on the number of registered voters in their district). In exchange for capping their total spending, these candidates qualify to receive matching funds from the Hawaii Election Campaign Fund, up to 10% of their expenditure limit.

question: What are the terms and election cycles for county offices in Hawaii?
question_variants: When are county mayors elected in HI?
Hawaii county official term lengths
Are county councils on the 2026 ballot?
answer: Elected county officials in Hawaii—including Mayors and County Councilmembers—serve either two-year or four-year terms depending on the specific county charter. They are elected during the standard even-year August primary and November general election cycles.

question: What is the canvassing and certification timeline for Hawaii elections?
question_variants: When are Hawaii election results official?
HI certification deadline
State Canvassing Board meeting Hawaii
answer: Because Hawaii processes mail ballots heavily, canvassing takes a few days. The chief election officer and county clerks must complete the canvass and certify the results within 5 days following the election. The final, official certification occurs after the expiration of the time allowed for bringing an election contest.

question: When and how are special elections called in Hawaii?
question_variants: Hawaii special election timeline
Who calls a special election to fill a US House vacancy in HI?
Filling a midterm vacancy Hawaii
answer: Special elections to fill federal or certain state vacancies are called by a proclamation from the Governor. For U.S. House vacancies, the Governor must issue a proclamation calling for a special election to be held on a date ensuring at least 60 days notice.
